They had four or five different concepts that they were putting together to get to, you know, a certain set of results here that looked that was like pretty reasonable. But you know the question that this team had was much bigger, and much broader you know so they were taking this one concept from NICE and combining it with one other concept. And so you know like 15,000 results, you know, and they're like, "I don't understand they only had, you know, 800 or whatever," you know, so it turned into a very long education session over multiple meetings. It took a ton of time. I mean, I probably spent a solid like 30 hours, you know, on that sucker, which is not how long I usually spend you know but it was working so hard to try to make this thing work. You know, it wasn't a matter of just beating your head against the wall or something like that, you know, but sometimes those are the interactions that take a lot more time. chunk_text: this was the spreadsheet that I put together for the grey literature sources that we couldn't export the results from. So that if if two people, basically like, kind of I use the same kind of way that screening is done. If two people said that it should be included, then it was included, if there's conflict, that I, I actually summarize those and send them to a third researcher who went through and made the the call whether they should be included or excluded. So that's kind of like a deep dive into one of my more involved workarounds that I have created.

chunk_text: Because of the lack of proximity/adjacency searching in PubMed, I usually am brainstorming all the different phrases or checking against the index list to see if shortened phrases will capture the longer phrases, ... I'll have a Word document up where I've got my PubMed search, and I'm literally typing up, translating one for one, and as I get to the phrases, I'll break them out and I'll see if the phrases versus adjacency/proximity searches for Medline,I'll break them out and I'll see if the phrases versus adjacency proximity searches for Medline make a difference. And that's kind of at the point where I decide if I'm going to stay with PubMed as my, my primary database or if I'm going to use Ovid MEDLINE, what I've for most of the questions I'm working with the proximity or adjacency searching hasn't been important enough to justify using often met switching and using Ovid MEDLINE specifically. And I have a personal opinion that it's better to and for more reproducibility to have a PubMed search that anybody could access and use versus Ovid MEDLINE.
code_1: Searching as art, concepts as materials

chunk_text: I find the EBSCO interface frustrating because of the way they've kind of tweaked their search interface that even if you are very particular, it kind of expands your search automatically. And even when you deselect it, you have to continually check to make sure it's deselected.

code_3: Systematic searchers have different needs than other types of searchers

chunk_text: since the beginning of COVID... our searches have gone through the roof, like the demand has gone through the roof. And that's resulting from the fact that a number of our faculty got big COVID-related grants to do reviews, the fact that people could not do their regular person to person research. So they turned to reviews. And some people actually found that their workload was reduced to the point that they could go back and find all projects that they'd never finished and dust them off and bring them back. So I've had in the last year, I've had five or six of those. And updating old searches like that is another big pain point... there are now new subject headings, you know that the algorithms have changed so much that you're not even with the same search, you're not getting the same results. And some of those results had gone up, you know, you started out with 1500. Now you're getting 905 years later, and you're going well, what's that about? Where did those other things go? No. So there's all kinds of things around updating searches, where in the end, two of them, when we went through what was going to change, said, no, we're going to start from square one. And we're going to, you know, do the whole rescreening right from the beginning, because there's just so much change that it would never be replicable.
code_1: Searching as art, concepts as materials

chunk_text: We're using this a lot, the "What kind of review should I do?" Because we get that question so many times. That takes you from the beginning of the "Why do you want to do a systematic review?" through to documenting who the author is. And it's all just hangs there with all of this supportive stuff. chunk_text: this is a field that is evolving all of the time. And there are people within the field who seem quite precious about sort of the original form of the field and would like things to stay the same... you can't be stuck in sort of the original definition of the systematic review and expect that that's going to continue to apply forever, because it's not, lots of things are changing within that. And even if you look at the the revised version of the Cochrane handbook, there are changes in there that, you know, that are different, right? Where the process is different. And I think people, you know, people kind of get it under their belt and think, Well, I'm done, I can just go ahead and do the job. But there's so much updating that you have to constantly do to keep yourself current and to, to make sure that when you do that kind of review and make that kind of decision that you're actually working in the same realm... if I were going to go do a health technology assessment, I would have to essentially go and retrain myself for a day to make sure that I knew what I was doing that was appropriate in that field.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

And so we teach once a month to try to get that knowledge across. So that's one of the big pain points is that that, you know, we end up just teaching constantly when we could be searching, right?

chunk_text: EBSCO has so many databases, and they are trying to optimize their system for cross database searching. So there are a lot of optional things that have got turned on. chunk_text: (I start my searching on Embase.com) Because of the thesaurus, it's much broader than the one in PubMed. And they have a lot more index terms. So if you start in Medline, there is a chance that you're going to miss certain index terms. So that's why you start in the, that's, that's how I was taught by my colleague, [NAME OF COLLEAGUE]. So start in Embase, and then the search for the index terms in the MeSH database. And sometimes you see, indeed, that Embase has like five index terms, and the mesh database has just one. And that's one of the things that I always say, that is one of the advantages of Embase. And one of the disadvantages of Embase.
code_1: Searching as art, concepts as materials

chunk_text: The index terms is something that I sometimes get very sad about. Especially now when I read a lot that they want to do more automatic term, adding automatic terms in not using humans anymore. So I that's the that's some that's something that sometimes it's not working correctly. Because if it's been automatically added, then the computer thinks for you, and then some, sometimes they use a index term that's really not relevant for the article. So that gives you more irrelevant articles for the researcher to plow through, even though it is totally unnecessary... that means we have to search everything in Title abstract. And the danger of only using title abstract is that you may sometimes not know the spelling differences, or they made a typo and in the abstract, and then you will never find the article that you're searching for.

chunk_text: (One workaround I have is) trying very hard on trying to see a research question from another angle. Once I had someone who had a question about what was it again? Oh, how many times medical circumcision was performed? But strangely enough, you can't really find that much articles about medical circumcisions. Usually, it's about with a religious undertone. I kind of flipped the question and searched for the diseases that were you that medical circumcision was used for? And how many times that happens in a in a population? Because if you know, if it's like 10%, then you know that it's almost 10% of the medical circumcision. So that that's what I mean, certainly looking at the research question from a different angle with the straightforward angle isn't working... But it's, it's just looking at the question in different way, and hope that you find an answer.

chunk_text: I think Ovid is the best. It has all the sort of search features you want in terms of being able to do proper phrase searching, proximity searching, being able to download the records easily without any volume limits that have to be changed. It enables, it's now got sort of an improved side search function, which makes it easier to update the saved searches than it used to in the past. So it's got a lot of the features that I think are essential to make systematic review searching, reasonably streamlined, it's still not perfect, the Safe Search could still be improved even more. And they've introduced, they've introduced some improvements, but it's still not, still a bit clunky to try and improve your searches and the search launch option where you can learn to launch a search from outside of it, in my experience sort of works some of the time but not all of the time. It depends where I'm logged in from, to help as to how far the search sort of like just runs nicely. So I like Ovid a lot.
code_1: Interfaces dictate and influence search processes

chunk_text: What I ideally want in an interface is the ability to combine concepts, and then play around with the concepts rather than the search terms, potentially. So with most of the database interfaces, that editing process where you recombine sets can be quite cumbersome to do and you can lose track of where you've got to. So something like 2DSearch, which is a visual presentation of the search concepts, and which allows you to move them around and combine them is a more helpful way of exploring search structures. When you've got a complicated search. You've got a simple search drug A versus drug B in a in a particular population, then you don't need anything fancy to play around with, you can quickly see what the concepts are and combine them. If you've got a more complicated topic, like a public health topic or something about barriers and facilitators to the uptake of a new drug or sort of fuzzy topics that are hard to capture. And you're playing around with several different concepts to try and see, which is the best combination, or series of combinations.  None of the interfaces are really good at letting you move large blocks of search terms around really easily and recombine and, and, and, and see what the impact of taking one one concept out in is and adding another in is. So interfaces that allow you to do that like 2dSearch are I think really helpful... So yeah, most of the main interfaces we use are limited when you're doing complicated searches and lots of set combination. And they don't show you visually how the combinations are working, which I think for a lot of people would be really helpful... It's more a way of deciding if you've got the best combinations of concepts before you then go away and run your search in some of the other databases... PubVenn's another package where it's an internet resource where you can type your search in in a crude way at the top, or in a detailed way. And it will show you visually, the Venn diagrams. And again, that can help with just seeing the relative sizes of the literature. And you can hover over the intersections so that you can look at the records that are missed if you don't find the records bang in the middle.
code_1: Interfaces dictate and influence search processes
code_3: Visualization of search concepts is better than command-line query builders

chunk_text: one of the pain points that I frequently encounter is so often I will look at strategies that other people have published saying a systematic review, but a lot of people will just put in a search block from PubMed, or one line very complicated search all in one line. So one of the things that I find most challenging is then disentangling those one line searches to see how the search is really constructed. So I think what would be really useful is something that decomposed searches. So it turned into a line by line strategy. Single Line strategies are a complete nightmare. Because you, you spend, you can spend, you know, an hour disentangling a complicated strategy that's presented in one line, and then then discover that the you don't agree with the logic or whatever, where you don't agree with their approach. And you've wasted an hour trying to understand why they did what they've done. So one-line strategies, you know, searches that are presented just as a one-line strategy are my real bugbear and some software that would disaggregate all the information and show you what they what they reverted back to a multi line strategy would be, would be really, really good.
code_1: Interfaces dictate and influence search processes
code_3: Architecture of search strategy (single vs multi-line)

chunk_text: as we discussed earlier, a search translator that could cope with all of the syntax conversions, and could also, what most of the search translators don't do is they don't check, they can't convert the subject headings, so they can't convert the MEDLINE into EMTREE, etc. And so, search translators that also took on that task, or started to help you to take on that task will also be really useful. But I've never seen so far anything that tries to help you with translating the subject indexing between different databases.

chunk_text: A lot of the consultancy companies that I work with, they don't have Embase for example. So sometimes they will buy access for for two weeks or a week to Embase and sometimes they will use the company they're working for as subscription and that could be in any of the interfaces, so a lot of it is driven by what my clients subscribe to, and what their budget is. And so sometimes we're looking at other ways to broaden In the searches to compensate for not searching Embase, so we're looking at using Google Scholar, we're looking at using Microsoft Academic, we'll be looking at AskAlex, whatever it's called, Open Alex. And then I've been looking, because I'm on my own, I can't afford to take subscriptions out to large numbers of databases. But I have been thinking about possibly taking out a subscription to to DimensionsAI, because they do offer for a reasonable amount of money, quite large, the option to download quite large sets of data, and because they have access to, you know, a wide range of literature, there is the chance that searching Dimensions might compensate to some extent for not being able to search Embase. So yeah, I'm trying to fill in people's gaps, because they're not going to take out subscriptions, or I'm trying to think more about offering them citation searching options.... there might be ways of filling in the gaps that you that are left by not doing the Embase search. So yeah, I mean, I make lots of recommendations to clients, they will make decisions based on on what they can afford, and also what they've budgeted for the search. Often they're employing me at the point where they've already decided how many days they can afford my time. So I can't really, you know, I can suggest 20 databases, but then there wouldn't be enough time for me to search 20 databases. So it's often easier when I'm doing work for people in universities, because then obviously, they have often have access to more resources, more databases, and they're more open to searching, lots of resources that might be useful, whereas most of the consultancy companies I work for, are trying to do the bare minimum of searches that will qualify as a systematic review.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: In your opinion, to what extent are your preferences regarding platforms considered when your library decides which ones to license? 08: when I started working, where I work now,  we did not have Medline through Ovid. And I was hired to be an evidence synthesis expert and librarian and I was basically like, we need to have this. Similarly with with Embase, we did have it but you know, in discussions about whether or not we should have it, we have a school of pharmacy. So that also factored into that decision, but when it has come up, you know, that that is a really essential source for reviews was part of that. When there was a discussion about Scopus and/or Web of Science, we got Scopus, maybe, maybe about 10 years ago. And there was some discussion at that point. And then when we were discussing not continuing with Web of Science, I certainly weighed in on that. I don't know how much it was actually a component. But, you know, I wasn't rooting to keep it for the sake of doing evidence syntheses. I think various other factors were also leaning towards us getting rid of it, but I certainly didn't have any arguments with that, because I don't like searching it. I haven't really pushed too hard on anything else, like any getting many other databases through Ovid. So I'm not really sure, that hasn't come up too much... we got to get used to searching what we have access to and not looking too much further.
code_1: Systematic searching occurs within the information market
code_3: Economics and politics of e-resources licensing

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: And what platforms do you find easiest and/or best suited for systematic searching? NW-11: Definitely Ovid databases. I just think you can have the most control. Andy Hickner: Are there a specific interface features that Ovid has that you find especially helpful? NW-11: I think just the fact that you have to tell it exactly what to do. chunk_text: We finished it up on Friday, I made sure I got everything in Covidence on Friday. I come in Monday to a message saying, you know, I think I'd like to limit it to 10 years and I'd also like to make this one other change. Andy Hickner: Oh, no. NW-12: Oh, yeah. Um, so I had to write back and say, You need to understand this is a really time consuming project. For me. I can't just do this in PubMed, I have to translate all the other data is bla bla bla. But of course if this is what you need done, we will do it for you. And she wrote back full of apologies and said that she really does need to have this done. And it's really not that much of a pain. The keywords that she needed taken in and out were minimal. But yes, for as point of pain goes, there's one. Having researchers ask me to re-upload everything to Covidence. Another point of pain for her was she was not good at reading my emails, I would send an email and then she'd shoot one back with asking questions that I had just answered in that email. I'm sure I'm not the only person to talk about that. She also didn't seem to care about being particular. When I introduce myself, when I'm working with new researchers, I tell them that I'm a bit of a perfectionist, and that this works well in systematic reviews, because we had to be very particular and find every potentially appropriate article, but that will probably drive them crazy, a little farther down the road. And she, I was driving her crazy from from the get go, she was the one with “autism or Asperger's?” "Oh, yeah, yeah, let's include that." She just wanted me to give her the articles. She didn't appreciate the process that it took to get there... She really, she wanted to go fast. She wanted. She just wanted these articles now. And I had a hard time conveying to her like the seriousness of this.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

chunk_text: She's writing a scoping review. She wanted to talk about friendship, peer friendships, peer mentorships, peer support groups, whatever, for adults with intellectual disabilities, when it comes to finding jobs. There's a lot of keywords out there that pertain to those, those are fuzzy topics. It's not like shoulder arthroscopy, you know, and she would just give me a couple and I would come back and say, Well, how about this one? And I ended up doing more citation checking with that. In PubMed, you know, there's a similar articles, I would check the similar articles to see if they were returned if they weren't, and I was one combing through the title in the abstract to see if there were additional keywords we could pick up and then I'd send her an email saying, what about these terms? And almost always it was “Oh, yeah.” So I guess maybe over-reliance on the librarian to come up with the correct terms.
code_1: Searching as art, concepts as materials

chunk_text: There's a lot of education that goes into working with people on these systematic reviews. Like the one I just described, this was this woman's first scoping review. And I think she just honestly didn't know all the things that went on. And even though I told her, you know, the -- One thing I tell people is the number of results that we get in PubMed will approximately double by the time we put everything into Covidence... And that just didn't sink in. And she was horrified at the number of results, which is why we had to go with the 10 years and blah, blah, blah. So I guess education is one of the biggest, maybe not workaround, one of the ways to just deal with it, I use what I call epic emails, I warn people, you're going to be getting epic emails from me with long search strings, ask them to run it, we’ll look at individual terms. So NOT one search set, a set of search results from the other. So we can see the terms of the results that are returned specifically by that. Those are usually in my epic emails. And I guess over-communication is what I'm trying to say here. Over-communication and education... Sometimes I'll complain and my boss will say, Yo, if you want me to go talk to somebody and tell them that's really just too much work. I've never, I've never had to invoke invoke a higher authority. We've always been able to figure it out. I guess, grin and bear it is probably my biggest workaround.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

chunk_text: One thing I tell people is the number of results that we get in PubMed will approximately double by the time we put everything into Covidence. And I think that's true. You know, every study is different. Sometimes it's more than double sometimes it's less than double, but just as a general guideline, you know, be prepared.
code_1: Interfaces dictate and influence search processes

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: Okay. Does the ease of searching a particular source factor in your selection of which sources to search? NW-12: No, it doesn't. I posted on one of the listservs, probably about two months ago when Google Scholar made its changes. I don't know if you use Google Scholar much, but it's much easier to export. But when you delete each citation individually, and they want you to label things every time you put something in your library, X out the label thing. And I posted and a couple people wrote back like "Yeah, it's really a pain. No, I haven't found a way around that yet." One person said "it makes me less likely to use Google Scholar." I was just kind of like, what? You, you would do that? So no, I don't think I don't think there is a platform that I don't use, because I don't like it.
code_1: Systematic searching occurs within the information market

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: To what extent are your preferences regarding platforms considered when your library decides which ones to license? NW-12: I would say to a pretty high degree. I actually came across PsycTests. We have PsycINFO and Psycarticles, but we don't have PsycTests. And I emailed our collections -- We only have two people in our collections department -- "So what do you guys know about this?" Like, "nothing, but I can get you a free trial." She got me a free trial. And we love it was actual tests in there. So now she's getting a quote, who knows if we'll be able to buy it or not. But I definitely feel if there's a resource that might be useful that it can be brought up and it will be looked into. I don't know about maintaining the ones we have. Sometimes I do think the Reference Librarians can be a bit more involved in weeding out the ones that aren't being used. We have usage stats. But I don't know, I don't get involved in that end of it all. I don't see the usage stats, and how much they cost, whether it's worth keeping something. So yeah, maybe we could be more involved in weeding the electronic collections.
code_1: Systematic searching occurs within the information market
code_3: Economics and politics of e-resources licensing

chunk_text: I do think it's helpful to start with MeSH terms, that's just my own particular way of going about things. I'll start with MeSH terms with the researcher. Like I was working on an autism study, and she didn't give you the keyword Asperger's. But that was there in the mesh tree, which brought up that conversation. chunk_text: Embase and Scopus are supposed to be pretty similar. And we have Scopus, and Embase has the EMTREE, which is like their MeSH terms from what I understand. But I think the EMTREE features would probably be nice to have, they might make my life a little bit easier. Using Embase and EMTREE instead of being a super perfectionist with my Scopus search string.

chunk_text: (reviewing intake form/checklist) I always go over this here, just to kind of explain to them. This is what the woman that I talked about the pain points with, this is what she did not get, I say that I was doing this search on feeding tubes, I have all these terms for feeding tubes, I missed this one. And the woman was not happy with me. And we had to have a discussion about, “I've probably never seen that phrase in my life. I'm not a clinician, that I need the researcher’s input.” I don't want them to think they got something from the library and it should be perfect. So I I like to end with this as a this is how we're going to be working together kind of thing.
code_1: Searching as art, concepts as materials

chunk_text: (shares detailed checklist that includes basic education about SR methods) Every time whenever -- even people that I've done systematic reviews with before, I'll fill out this form. Have you determined there's no existing systematic review? Have you checked Prospero? What's your research question? Is this a team effort? Every now and then we get a student who comes in who wants to do a systematic review for their assignment that's due in three months. Yeah. Are you following Prisma? Do you plan to use Covidence? Or something else? These are just things that help help me know what's coming down the pike. Which databases do you want to search? You plan to publish? How will the librarian be given credit? I have found that if you ask this upfront, and this initial review, then it makes it a whole lot easier when it comes to wrapping things up... Expected timeline for this part of the systematic review, I could send this to you if you Want. Do you have any of these limitations? Tell me about one article in this field
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

chunk_text: (shows table with concepts broken out - Population, Procedure, Qualifications on Procedure) I always work with a chart, it doesn't have to be a PICO chart. But when I'm working with them on terms, this is so much easier to follow than this (referring to the search strategy)… And I will tell that to people, I'm a visual person, this is just easier for me to see. And they really appreciate not having to go through all of this and figure out what's what.
code_1: Interfaces dictate and influence search processes
code_3: Visualization of search concepts is better than command-line query builders

chunk_text: I prefer PubMed to Ovid because I think you can share the results easier... primarily PubMed, I'd say 95% of the time.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: What other methods and tools do you use to translate the initial strategy to other databases? 12: I don't really have one. I really like using title/abstract as a restriction in PubMed. Of course, that's ti AB. Scopus, of course, you know, it's title-abs in front of the Boolean set of terms. Really, the only one I use is word's Find and Replace, I will find and replace ti A B and replace it with nothing. And then Scopus uses a squiggly brackets instead of parentheses. Again, I use Word to find and replace that. But I don't have any slick AI application that helps me with that translation. If you can cover that in your article, you got a guaranteed reader here.
code_1: Context reveals searcher needs
code_3: Staying current amidst constant change (This code is latent in this instance - NW-12 is unaware of Polyglot translator)

chunk_text: Andy Hickner: And what other platforms or databases do you routinely search for us our projects? NW-12: Scopus, we don't have Embase. So we have Scopus, CINAHL. Google Scholar, I don't know if that counts --(AH: It does) --Those are kind of the "always," and then I might do ERIC, if it's an educational one. PsycINFO, I do a lot of PsycINFO. Oh, and the Cochrane databases, Cochrane central register of controlled trials.
code_1: Systematic searching occurs within the information market

chunk_text: I don't like CINAHL, or the EBSCO platform as well, for a couple of reasons. They don't have title or abstract, you have to search it all in title, do it again, and abstract and or it together, and CINAHL-all also often breaks. I can give someone a half a page worth of PubMed search and say, copy and paste this, it works in PubMed, it works in Scopus, as long as they go into the advanced search. It's not going to work in CINAHL, drives me crazy. And it's also a pain, you can't just do S2 and S3 and S4, that's not a reproducible search, you have to copy it, paste it into the box, copy the next one, paste it into the box.
